P01: P01 cooks rice, soup, meat-based dishes, and at one point uses a food processor and a yeast-based step. Their actions are very often about dislodging stuck contents — small pieces of rice, onion, food, spice, soup, vinegar, oil, or liquid that have stuck to a spoon, spatula, strainer, ladle, or lid, which they hit, shake, or tap to drop back into the appropriate pot, pan, plate, bowl, bin, or container. They protect surfaces (lid placed upside down on the counter so it doesn't wet the surface), open the oven to check trays, slide gloves onto a hand before handling something hot, and operate a boiler/food-processor panel. They are stationary, cook from memory, and do not consult a phone or recipe. Cleanup is not a major thread of their cooking.
P02: P02 is following a written recipe — they consult the recipe sheet several times during the session to see what the next step is or how long the recipe recommends cooking something for — while baking (raspberry-lemon curd, frangipane, cheese straws, quorn packets, pastry, muffin trays, baking trays) and doing parallel savory prep (onion peeling, potato cutting, garlic, tomatoes, mushrooms, bacon, squash bottle from the fridge). Their kitchen has a 'washing-up bowl' that recurs as infrastructure — the tap fills it, water goes into it, hands shake water into it. Many of their actions are explained in terms of two-handed choreography that names a specific hand (freeing the right or left hand, holding the frying pan in left hand while stirring with the spatula in right hand). They repeatedly reach into bags to grab the next packet of an ingredient and go in and out of the fridge.
P03: P03 is a high-tempo cook making a savory meal and reasons explicitly about ingredient state — olive oil too hot, garlic cloves burning, spring greens just ready, kale to cook, blueberries to wash, potatoes to cook evenly, mushrooms to mix with garlic, coffee to brew evenly, aubergine and its squeezed-out sunflower oil. Their kitchen has a dishwasher that is part of the active workflow during cooking — the cheese grater, metal colander, glass lid, and other items are actively placed into the dishwasher's top or bottom rack, and they switch the washing-machine program on a dial. Many of their actions are about making room or clearing the way for the next item they're holding in the other hand (the pizza oven, the bowl, the plastic colander, the chopping board, the spanish omelette). They protect surfaces — oily side of the spatula facing up so it doesn't touch the kitchen top, lid placed so oil doesn't spill, chopping board out of the way before picking up the colander.
P04: P04 is weighing and measuring ingredients on a kitchen scale — bowls and tumblers are repeatedly placed on the scale to weigh rice, fennel seeds, water, anise flour, and other ingredients, and water is poured between a tumbler and a measuring jug multiple times. They are preparing dosa batter (with chillies and lentils shallow-fried in the pan), scrambled eggs, and a lemon sauce. They use 'in order to' phrasing throughout. Many of their actions are about (a) getting rid of excess water from pans, plates, hands, spatulas, spoons, or measuring jugs, often by shaking, tipping, or inverting; (b) deferred dishwashing — placing knives, pans, spatulas, or tablespoons down 'to be washed later' or 'kept for washing,' lathering soap on hands, washing handles under running tap water with the right hand, and letting washed plates and bowls dry on the dish drainer beside the sink; (c) inverting or rotating bowls and plates to expose the back side or pour contents out.
P05: P05 is making porridge, pesto (with a mortar and pestle), curry (with frozen spinach, coconut milk, and boiling water added), pasta on the side, and chops a banana — and the spoon, wooden spoon, teaspoon, spatula, and pan are their core tools. Their characteristic answer is 'to get rid of excess [specific substance] off the pan' where they name the substance currently in the pan: excess porridge, excess pesto, excess curry, excess mango chutney, excess coconut milk, excess cheese, excess oil. They reason about counter cleanliness — placing spoons or chopping boards down in ways that don't make the counter dirty or messy — and they explain hand transitions by what the freed hand is about to do next (open the microwave, take off an oven glove on the left hand, pick up more things, fill up the kettle, turn on the cold tap, slide the onion into the pan with the knife). They taste-test by licking the spoon and say so directly ('because I like the taste of peanut butter,' 'because the pesto tastes nice').
P06: P06 is making pancakes and coffee in this session (small sample, treat as low-confidence). Their actions are explained in terms of pancake-specific concerns (making the batter smooth, regulating the gas-stove knob so the pancake doesn't burn), pouring coffee or milk between cups and from the frying pan into the saucepan, mixing spices together in a bowl, and rubbing flour off their hands. They handle doors meaningfully — entering the living room, accessing the kitchen — and they operate the gas-stove knob, the water tap to wash the frying pan and tongs, and the dustbin lid. They tilt plates to drop onion peels into the dustbin while holding the bin lid in the other hand.
P07: P07 is cooking a savory multi-component meal — chickpeas with olive oil and spices, a mushroom-and-rice dish in a frying pan, sweet potatoes tipped from a bowl onto a baking tray, butter scraped from a spatula into the frying pan, avocado tapped into a canister, onions, a kettle of water — and they actively clean during cooking: rinsing the saucepan underside under the running tap, pouring boiling water to clean the sink, moving a cloth to clean the kitchen island, rinsing both hands of soap, and turning on the dishwasher. Many of their actions are about directing ingredients to the right destination (the last drop of olive oil onto the chickpeas, oil into the large frying pan, the cheese into the bowl, the onions into the frying pan, the avocado into the canister, the rice and mushrooms back into the pan). They explain stirring as keeping the frying pan moving to stop it from burning, and they make room for the saucepan, tray, or colander by moving the colander or other items aside.
P08: P08 is running two parallel workflows that are equally important: nutrition-tracking via a phone app, and active dishwashing with soap. The phone-app workflow is real and frequent — they explicitly record, update, log, find, or begin measuring the nutritional value or measurements of named ingredients (carrot, olive oil, balsamic glaze, coriander, cucumber, chicken, chicken thighs, chilli, green chilli pepper, spice, salad, couscous, spaghetti, vegetables) in the app via the phone screen, keyboard, or camera, and they zero or adjust the kitchen scale before weighing. Separately, they actively clean and wash items with soap during cooking — knives, cutting boards, plates, spatulas, bowls, pans, the top of the pot, the colander, coffee mug — using phrases like 'to clean,' 'to clean off soap,' 'to clean from soap,' 'to clean up the space,' 'to clean with water,' 'to clean with both hands,' and they dry items with a tea towel (hands, fingers, the kitchen scale, cutting board, plate, spatula, top of the pot). They pinch carrots, onions, and cucumbers to steady them on the cutting board before slicing with the knife in their right hand. Both phone-logging answers AND cleaning/drying answers are genuine GT for P08 and should not be treated as generic distractors.
P09: P09 is preparing a potato-chicken-cheese dish that involves breadcrumbs and forms breaded balls (the 'potato chicken ball' is dipped in egg and placed on foil), alongside curry with hibiscus drink filtered through a slotted spoon, mango scooped from the skin, and a Spanish omelette. They are the only cook who walks across the kitchen and across the room repeatedly — to peel and slice potatoes, to retrieve a knife from the knife stand, to rinse a tablespoon, to discard eggshells, to pick up the scale. Their actions are dominated by transfers and pick-ups between distinct stations — counter, sink, hob, cupboard, drawer, knife rack, drying rack, fridge, washing basket — paired with continuous interleaved cleaning using a sponge, washing-up liquid, and a hand towel (scrubbing the pot/knife/serving spoon/peeler/ice cube tray, rinsing the sponge, wiping the counter surface, washing bowls and plates in the sink). They almost always explain an action by naming the specific concrete next object or step they're about to do — pick up the sponge, pick up the bottle of washing-up liquid, place the bowl, turn on the tap, scrub the knife, stir the onions, chop the chicken, dip the potato-chicken ball in egg, retrieve the serving spoon, close the cupboard, return the breadcrumbs. When multiple answer choices use 'so I can' planning phrasing, the right one is the one that names a specific concrete next object or step, not a vague motive.
